name: Spanish B1 Grammar
description: Master CEFR B1 Spanish grammar — from the present subjunctive and its triggers (espero que, cuando, para que) to the conditional, future, pluperfect, double object pronouns, and verbal periphrases. Fill in the blank to prove you know which form fits. Scoped from the Instituto Cervantes PCIC B1 curriculum.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} pronto.
(I hope you arrive soon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you arrive soon.
facts.explanation: llegues — present subjunctive of llegar (tú). After espero que, use the subjunctive. -ar subjunctive pattern: hablar → hable, hables, hable... Note: llegar → llegue (not *llegues for yo/él forms).

facts.quizQuestion:
Espera hasta que {___} Juan.
(Wait until Juan arrives.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Wait until Juan arrives.
facts.explanation: llegue — present subjunctive of llegar (él/ella/usted). Hasta que requires subjunctive when referring to a future event. Same form as yo: llegue.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} con él.
(I want you to talk to him.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to talk to him.
facts.explanation: hables — present subjunctive of hablar (tú). Quiero que requires subjunctive in the subordinate clause. Pattern: hablar → hable, hables, hable, hablemos, habléis, hablen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es importante que {___} claramente.
(It's important that we speak clearly.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that we speak clearly.
facts.explanation: hablemos — present subjunctive of hablar (nosotros). Impersonal es importante que triggers subjunctive. Nosotros form always ends in -emos for -ar verbs.

facts.quizQuestion:
No quiero que {___} tan alto.
(I don't want them to speak so loudly.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't want them to speak so loudly.
facts.explanation: hablen — present subjunctive of hablar (ellos/ellas/ustedes). After no quiero que, the subjunctive is required. Third person plural: hablen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es necesario que {___} más.
(It's necessary that you work more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's necessary that you work more.
facts.explanation: trabajes — present subjunctive of trabajar (tú). Es necesario que triggers subjunctive. Regular -ar pattern: trabajar → trabaje, trabajes, trabaje.

facts.quizQuestion:
Prefiero que {___} en silencio.
(I prefer that he work in silence.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I prefer that he work in silence.
facts.explanation: trabaje — present subjunctive of trabajar (él/ella/usted). Prefiero que requires subjunctive. Note: same form for yo and él/ella in the present subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que no {___} nada malo.
(I hope nothing bad happens.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope nothing bad happens.
facts.explanation: pase — present subjunctive of pasar (él/ella/ello). After espero que, subjunctive required. Regular -ar: pasar → pase, pases, pase.

facts.quizQuestion:
Cuando {___}, estaré en casa.
(When you call, I'll be at home.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you call, I'll be at home.
facts.explanation: llames — present subjunctive of llamar (tú). Cuando + subjunctive is used when referring to a future event. Compare: Cuando llamas siempre estoy durmiendo (present indicative for habitual past action).

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} más verduras.
(I want you to eat more vegetables.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to eat more vegetables.
facts.explanation: comas — present subjunctive of comer (tú). Quiero que requires subjunctive. -er subjunctive pattern: comer → coma, comas, coma, comamos, comáis, coman (opposite vowel: -ar verbs use -e, -er/-ir verbs use -a).

facts.quizQuestion:
Es bueno que {___} fruta cada día.
(It's good that he eats fruit every day.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's good that he eats fruit every day.
facts.explanation: coma — present subjunctive of comer (él/ella/usted/yo). Es bueno que triggers subjunctive. Note: yo and él/ella/usted have the same form in the present subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
No quiero que {___} tanto café.
(I don't want you to drink so much coffee.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't want you to drink so much coffee.
facts.explanation: bebas — present subjunctive of beber (tú). No quiero que triggers subjunctive. -er pattern: beber → beba, bebas, beba, bebamos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} mucho en este curso.
(I hope you learn a lot in this course.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you learn a lot in this course.
facts.explanation: aprendas — present subjunctive of aprender (tú). After espero que, subjunctive required. aprender → aprenda, aprendas, aprenda, aprendamos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} cerca de mí.
(I want you to live close to me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to live close to me.
facts.explanation: vivas — present subjunctive of vivir (tú). Quiero que requires subjunctive. -ir pattern: vivir → viva, vivas, viva, vivamos, viváis, vivan.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es posible que {___} en España.
(It's possible that he lives in Spain.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's possible that he lives in Spain.
facts.explanation: viva — present subjunctive of vivir (él/ella/yo). Es posible que triggers subjunctive. Note: viva is the same form for both yo and él/ella/usted.

facts.quizQuestion:
Te pido que {___} la respuesta.
(I ask you to write the answer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I ask you to write the answer.
facts.explanation: escribas — present subjunctive of escribir (tú). Te pido que requires subjunctive. escribir → escriba, escribas, escriba.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} haga buen tiempo mañana.
(I hope the weather is good tomorrow.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope the weather is good tomorrow.
facts.explanation: ojalá — from Arabic 'inshallah' (if God wills). Ojalá always requires the subjunctive. It expresses a wish or hope. Ojalá + present subjunctive = I hope... (possible wish).

facts.quizQuestion:
Te lo explico {___} lo entiendas.
(I'll explain it so that you understand.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'll explain it so that you understand.
facts.explanation: para que — used to express purpose. Para que ALWAYS requires the subjunctive. Contrast with para + infinitive (same subject): estudio para aprender vs. te explico para que aprendas (different subjects).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llegues, llámame.
(When you arrive, call me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you arrive, call me.
facts.explanation: cuando — when referring to a future event, cuando requires the subjunctive. Compare: Cuando llegué (past — indicative preterite) vs. Cuando llegues (future — subjunctive). This distinction is a classic B1 trap.

facts.quizQuestion:
Llámame {___} salgas.
(Call me before you leave.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Call me before you leave.
facts.explanation: antes de que — always requires the subjunctive. Compare with antes de + infinitive (same subject): Me ducho antes de salir. vs. Llámame antes de que salgas (different subjects → antes de que + subjunctive).

facts.quizQuestion:
No saldré {___} termines.
(I won't leave until you finish.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I won't leave until you finish.
facts.explanation: hasta que — requires subjunctive when referring to a future/hypothetical event. No saldré hasta que termines = I won't leave until you finish (future event). Compare: Esperé hasta que terminó (past — indicative).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estés aquí.
(I'm glad you're here.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm glad you're here.
facts.explanation: me alegro de que — expressions of emotion always require the subjunctive when there are two different subjects. Me alegro de que + subjunctive = I'm glad that... Other emotion triggers: siento que, me gusta que, tengo miedo de que.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} sea verdad.
(I don't think it's true.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't think it's true.
facts.explanation: no creo que — expressions of doubt/denial require the subjunctive. Creo que + indicative (belief), but no creo que + subjunctive (doubt). Critical B1 contrast: Creo que es verdad vs. No creo que sea verdad.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estudies cada día.
(It's important that you study every day.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that you study every day.
facts.explanation: es importante que — impersonal expressions of obligation/value always require the subjunctive. Common B1 triggers: es importante que, es necesario que, es posible que, es mejor que, es bueno que, es una pena que.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llueva mañana.
(It's possible that it will rain tomorrow.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's possible that it will rain tomorrow.
facts.explanation: es posible que — possibility expressions require the subjunctive. Compare: quizás/tal vez + subjunctive (same idea). Contrast: es seguro que + indicative (certainty triggers indicative).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} venga hoy.
(I doubt he'll come today.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I doubt he'll come today.
facts.explanation: dudo que — verbs of doubt always require subjunctive. dudo que = I doubt that. Other doubt triggers: no pienso que, no creo que, no es verdad que, es imposible que.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tengas suerte.
(I hope you have luck.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you have luck.
facts.explanation: espero que — one of the most common B1 subjunctive triggers. Espero que is a verb of volition (wanting/hoping). Always followed by subjunctive. esperar que = to hope that / to expect that.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} vengas conmigo.
(I want you to come with me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to come with me.
facts.explanation: quiero que — the most iconic B1 subjunctive trigger. Verbs of wanting/wishing require subjunctive when the subjects differ: Quiero IR (same subject — infinitive) vs. Quiero que VENGAS (different subjects — subjunctive).

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} feliz.
(I hope you're happy.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you're happy.
facts.explanation: seas — present subjunctive of ser (tú). ser is highly irregular: sea, seas, sea, seamos, seáis, sean. No resemblance to the present indicative (soy, eres...). After espero que, subjunctive required.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} perfecto.
(I want it to be perfect.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want it to be perfect.
facts.explanation: sea — present subjunctive of ser (él/ella/usted and yo). Quiero que requires subjunctive. ser subjunctive: sea, seas, sea, seamos, seáis, sean. Completely irregular — no stem from infinitive or indicative forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es importante que {___} honestos.
(It's important that we be honest.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that we be honest.
facts.explanation: seamos — present subjunctive of ser (nosotros). Es importante que triggers subjunctive. Full paradigm: sea, seas, sea, seamos, seáis, sean.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} bien.
(I hope you're well.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you're well.
facts.explanation: estés — present subjunctive of estar (tú). estar subjunctive: esté, estés, esté, estemos, estéis, estén. Note the accent marks on all forms (estÉ, estÉS...). After espero que, subjunctive required.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es importante que {___} aquí a las ocho.
(It's important that you be here at eight.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that you be here at eight.
facts.explanation: esté — present subjunctive of estar (él/ella/usted/yo). Note: the written accent distinguishes esté (subjunctive) from este (demonstrative adjective). Es importante que requires subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} cuidado.
(I want you to be careful.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to be careful.
facts.explanation: tengas — present subjunctive of tener (tú). Tener is irregular: tenga, tengas, tenga, tengamos, tengáis, tengan. The stem is teng- (same as yo present indicative tengo → drop -o, add subjunctive endings).

facts.quizQuestion:
Es importante que {___} paciencia.
(It's important that you have patience.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that you have patience.
facts.explanation: tenga — present subjunctive of tener (él/ella/usted/yo). Pattern: take yo present indicative tengo → drop -o → teng- → add subjunctive vowel: tenga, tengas, tenga, tengamos, tengáis, tengan.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} los deberes.
(I want you to do your homework.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to do your homework.
facts.explanation: hagas — present subjunctive of hacer (tú). hago (yo present) → drop -o → hag- + subjunctive: haga, hagas, haga, hagamos, hagáis, hagan. Quiero que requires subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es necesario que {___} algo.
(It's necessary that we do something.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's necessary that we do something.
facts.explanation: haga — present subjunctive of hacer (él/ella/usted/yo). Es necesario que triggers subjunctive. haga is also used as usted affirmative imperative: ¡Hágalo! (Do it!).

facts.quizQuestion:
Cuando {___} a Madrid, visítame.
(When you go to Madrid, visit me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you go to Madrid, visit me.
facts.explanation: vayas — present subjunctive of ir (tú). ir is completely irregular: vaya, vayas, vaya, vayamos, vayáis, vayan. Cuando + future reference requires subjunctive. Note: vaya, vayamos are also nosotros/usted imperative forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} a la fiesta.
(I hope they go to the party.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope they go to the party.
facts.explanation: vayan — present subjunctive of ir (ellos/ellas/ustedes). Ojalá always requires subjunctive. ir subjunctive: vaya, vayas, vaya, vayamos, vayáis, vayan.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} pronto.
(I hope you come soon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you come soon.
facts.explanation: vengas — present subjunctive of venir (tú). vengo (yo) → drop -o → veng- + subjunctive: venga, vengas, venga, vengamos, vengáis, vengan. After espero que, subjunctive required.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} ayudarme.
(I hope you can help me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you can help me.
facts.explanation: puedas — present subjunctive of poder (tú). poder has a stem change: pued- (stem changes e→ue). pueda, puedas, pueda, podamos, podáis, puedan (note: nosotros/vosotros keep the -o- stem).

facts.quizQuestion:
Es posible que {___} venir.
(It's possible that he can come.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's possible that he can come.
facts.explanation: pueda — present subjunctive of poder (él/ella/usted/yo). Es posible que requires subjunctive. poder subjunctive stem: pued- (stem change e→ue except nosotros/vosotros).

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} la respuesta.
(I hope you know the answer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you know the answer.
facts.explanation: sepas — present subjunctive of saber (tú). saber is irregular: sepa, sepas, sepa, sepamos, sepáis, sepan. The irregular stem sep- comes from sé (yo present indicative). After espero que, subjunctive required.

facts.quizQuestion:
No quiero que le {___} nada.
(I don't want you to say anything to him.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't want you to say anything to him.
facts.explanation: digas — present subjunctive of decir (tú). digo (yo) → drop -o → dig- + subjunctive: diga, digas, diga, digamos, digáis, digan. No quiero que requires subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} ahora.
(I want you to leave now.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want you to leave now.
facts.explanation: salgas — present subjunctive of salir (tú). salgo (yo) → drop -o → salg- + subjunctive: salga, salgas, salga, salgamos, salgáis, salgan. Quiero que requires subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es importante que {___} atención.
(It's important that you pay attention.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that you pay attention.
facts.explanation: pongas — present subjunctive of poner (tú). pongo (yo) → drop -o → pong- + subjunctive: ponga, pongas, ponga, pongamos, pongáis, pongan. Es importante que requires subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} participar.
(I hope they want to participate.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope they want to participate.
facts.explanation: quieran — present subjunctive of querer (ellos). querer has stem change e→ie: quiera, quieras, quiera, queramos, queráis, quieran (nosotros/vosotros keep -e- stem). Ojalá requires subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tan alto, por favor.
(Don't speak so loudly, please.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't speak so loudly, please.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: hablar → no hables
facts.explanation: no hables — negative tú imperative of hablar. Negative imperatives use the present subjunctive. Contrast: habla (affirmative tú, A2) vs. no hables (negative tú, B1). Pattern: -ar → no + -es; -er/-ir → no + -as.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tan rápido.
(Don't eat so fast.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't eat so fast.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: comer → no comas
facts.explanation: no comas — negative tú imperative of comer. -er verb: use subjunctive form (coma → comas for tú). Affirmative: come. Negative: no comas. The switch from affirmative to subjunctive for negative is a key B1 point.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} con lápiz.
(Don't write in pencil.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't write in pencil.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: escribir → no escribas
facts.explanation: no escribas — negative tú imperative of escribir. -ir verb: use subjunctive form (escriba → escribas for tú). Affirmative: escribe. Negative: no escribas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} solo.
(Don't go alone.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't go alone.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: ir → no vayas
facts.explanation: no vayas — negative tú imperative of ir (irregular). Affirmative: ve (irregular). Negative: no vayas (uses subjunctive vaya → vayas). Complete contrast: ve al banco / no vayas al banco.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tan serio.
(Don't be so serious.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't be so serious.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: ser → no seas
facts.explanation: no seas — negative tú imperative of ser (irregular). Affirmative: sé. Negative: no seas (uses subjunctive sea → seas). Key irregular pair: sé / no seas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} miedo.
(Don't be afraid.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't be afraid.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: tener → no tengas
facts.explanation: no tengas — negative tú imperative of tener (irregular). Affirmative: ten. Negative: no tengas (uses subjunctive tenga → tengas). Irregular pair: ten / no tengas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} eso.
(Don't do that.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't do that.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: hacer → no hagas
facts.explanation: no hagas — negative tú imperative of hacer (irregular). Affirmative: haz. Negative: no hagas (uses subjunctive haga → hagas). Irregular pair: haz / no hagas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} mentiras.
(Don't tell lies.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't tell lies.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: decir → no digas
facts.explanation: no digas — negative tú imperative of decir (irregular). Affirmative: di. Negative: no digas (uses subjunctive diga → digas). Irregular pair: di / no digas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tarde.
(Don't come late.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't come late.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: venir → no vengas
facts.explanation: no vengas — negative tú imperative of venir (irregular). Affirmative: ven. Negative: no vengas (uses subjunctive venga → vengas). Irregular pair: ven / no vengas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} sin abrigo.
(Don't go out without a coat.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't go out without a coat.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative tú imperative: salir → no salgas
facts.explanation: no salgas — negative tú imperative of salir (irregular). Affirmative: sal. Negative: no salgas (uses subjunctive salga → salgas). Irregular pair: sal / no salgas.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tan deprisa, por favor.
(Please don't speak so fast.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Please don't speak so fast.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative usted imperative: hablar → no hable
facts.explanation: no hable — negative usted imperative of hablar. Usted imperatives (both affirmative and negative) use the subjunctive form: hable (affirmative) / no hable (negative). Same form, different polarity.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}.
(Don't worry.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't worry.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative usted imperative: reflexive (no se preocupe)
facts.explanation: no se preocupe — negative usted imperative of preocuparse. Reflexive verbs keep their pronoun before the negated form: No se preocupe (usted). Contrast: preocúpese (affirmative, attached) vs. no se preocupe (negative, before verb).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} todos a la vez.
(Don't all speak at once.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't all speak at once.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative ustedes imperative: hablar → no hablen
facts.explanation: no hablen — negative ustedes imperative of hablar. Ustedes imperative = subjunctive form: hablen (affirmative and negative). No hablen = don't (all of you) speak.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} de eso ahora.
(Let's not talk about that now.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's not talk about that now.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative nosotros imperative: hablar → no hablemos
facts.explanation: no hablemos — negative nosotros imperative. Affirmative: hablemos (let's talk). Negative: no hablemos (let's not talk). The nosotros subjunctive form is used for both.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} más despacio, por favor.
(Please speak more slowly.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Please speak more slowly.
facts.explanation: hable — affirmative usted imperative of hablar. Usted imperative = present subjunctive form. -ar verb: hable (not habla, which is tú affirmative). Used in formal contexts, customer service, professional settings.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} a las tres.
(Come at three o'clock.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Come at three o'clock.
facts.explanation: venga — affirmative usted imperative of venir (irregular). Uses subjunctive form: venga. Contrast with tú: ven (affirmative tú, A2) vs. venga usted. Vengan is the ustedes form.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} cuidado.
(Be careful.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Be careful.
facts.explanation: tenga — affirmative usted imperative of tener. Uses subjunctive form. Common fixed phrase: ¡Tenga cuidado! (Be careful!). Contrast tú: ten (short irregular affirmative) vs. usted: tenga.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} su nombre.
(Say your name.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Say your name.
facts.explanation: diga — affirmative usted imperative of decir. Uses subjunctive form (diga). Also heard when answering the phone in Spanish: ¿Diga? (Hello?). Tú affirmative: di vs. usted: diga.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} el favor de esperar.
(Please wait.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Please wait.
facts.explanation: haga — affirmative usted imperative of hacer. Uses subjunctive form. Haga el favor de + infinitivo is a polite way to make requests. Tú affirmative: haz vs. usted: haga.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} con el profesor.
(Talk to the professor.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Talk to the professor.
facts.explanation: hablen — affirmative ustedes imperative of hablar. Ustedes imperative = subjunctive form for ustedes: hablen. Used throughout Latin America as the formal/plural 'you all'. Vosotros would say: hablad.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} mañana, por favor.
(Come tomorrow, please.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Come tomorrow, please.
facts.explanation: vengan — affirmative ustedes imperative of venir. Uses subjunctive form: vengan (ellos/ustedes subjunctive). Contrast: ven (tú), venga (usted), vengan (ustedes).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} de esto luego.
(Let's talk about this later.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's talk about this later.
facts.explanation: hablemos — nosotros imperative (let's speak). Uses present subjunctive nosotros form. Equivalent to vamos a hablar. Both are common. Negative: no hablemos (let's not talk).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} juntos.
(Let's eat together.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's eat together.
facts.explanation: comamos — nosotros imperative of comer (let's eat). -er verb subjunctive nosotros: comamos. Alternative: vamos a comer (more colloquial). The nosotros imperative is more formal/literary.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} al parque.
(Let's go to the park.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's go to the park.
facts.grammarPointLabel: affirmative nosotros imperative: ir → vayamos (or vamos)
facts.explanation: vayamos — nosotros imperative of ir (formal). In practice, vamos is used instead of vayamos for affirmative. Negative: no vayamos (never *no vamos for negative imperative). This is an important distinction.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} con ella, pero no tengo tiempo.
(I would talk to her, but I don't have time.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I would talk to her, but I don't have time.
facts.explanation: hablaría — simple conditional of hablar (yo). Formation: infinitive + endings: -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían. All verbs use the same endings. Regular verbs add to full infinitive: hablar + ía = hablaría.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} más, pero estoy lleno.
(I would eat more, but I'm full.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I would eat more, but I'm full.
facts.explanation: comería — simple conditional of comer (yo). comer + ía = comería. Note: -er/-ir verbs add endings directly to infinitive, just like -ar verbs. The endings always have an accent: -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} en España si tuviera dinero.
(I would live in Spain if I had money.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I would live in Spain if I had money.
facts.explanation: viviría — simple conditional of vivir (yo). vivir + ía = viviría. This is a type 2 si-clause structure (B2+): si tuviera... + conditional. For now, note the conditional form viviría.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} con él si pudieras?
(Would you talk to him if you could?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Would you talk to him if you could?
facts.explanation: hablarías — simple conditional of hablar (tú). hablar + ías = hablarías. Tú conditional ends in -ías (with accent). Useful for hypothetical questions: ¿Qué harías? ¿Adónde irías?

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Qué {___} en mi lugar?
(What would you do in my place?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: What would you do in my place?
facts.explanation: haría — conditional of hacer (yo). hacer drops -ce and adds r: har- + ía = haría. Irregular conditional stems for hacer: har-. Full paradigm: haría, harías, haría, haríamos, haríais, harían.

facts.quizQuestion:
Yo no {___} eso.
(I wouldn't do that.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wouldn't do that.
facts.grammarPointLabel: simple conditional irregular: hacer → yo: haría
facts.explanation: haría — conditional of hacer (yo). Note: the question 'Yo no haría eso' literally says 'I (yo) wouldn't do that.' har- stem + ía. Same irregular stems for future: haré, harás... and conditional: haría, harías...

facts.quizQuestion:
No {___} eso si fuera tú.
(I wouldn't say that if I were you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wouldn't say that if I were you.
facts.explanation: diría — conditional of decir (yo). decir drops -ec and adds r: dir- + ía = diría. dir- stem: diría, dirías, diría, diríamos, diríais, dirían.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} tiempo para ayudarme?
(Would you have time to help me?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Would you have time to help me?
facts.explanation: tendrías — conditional of tener (tú). tener inserts -d-: tendr- + ías = tendrías. Same stem pattern: tendría, tendrías, tendría, tendríamos, tendríais, tendrían. Also future: tendré, tendrás...

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} hablar más despacio?
(Could you speak more slowly?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Could you speak more slowly?
facts.grammarPointLabel: simple conditional irregular: poder → podrías (polite request)
facts.explanation: podrías — conditional of poder (tú). poder drops -e-: podr- + ías = podrías. Used for polite requests in B1: ¿Podrías...? = Could you...? Very common in service interactions. Formal: ¿Podría usted...?

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} ayudarme, por favor?
(Could you help me, please?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Could you help me, please?
facts.explanation: podría — conditional of poder (usted/él/yo). podr- + ía = podría. ¿Podría...? (usted) is the most formal way to make a polite request. Same form as yo: Yo podría hacerlo (I could do it).

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} a la fiesta?
(Would you come to the party?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Would you come to the party?
facts.explanation: vendrías — conditional of venir (tú). venir inserts -d-: vendr- + ías = vendrías. Same stem in future: vendré. Full paradigm: vendría, vendrías, vendría, vendríamos, vendríais, vendrían.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} descansar más.
(You should rest more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: You should rest more.
facts.explanation: deberías — conditional of deber (tú), used for giving advice. Deberías + infinitive = you should... This is the standard B1 construction for advice: Deberías comer menos. Deberías estudiar más. (Not: Debes, which sounds like an order.)

facts.quizQuestion:
Mañana {___} con mi jefe.
(Tomorrow I will talk to my boss.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Tomorrow I will talk to my boss.
facts.explanation: hablaré — future simple of hablar (yo). Formation: infinitive + endings: -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án. Regular verbs attach endings to the full infinitive: hablar + é = hablaré.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} juntos el domingo.
(We will eat together on Sunday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We will eat together on Sunday.
facts.explanation: comeremos — future simple of comer (nosotros). comer + emos = comeremos. Note: nosotros future ends in -emos (same as present for -er verbs but with accent on e in future: comerEmos vs. comEmos present).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} en esta ciudad para siempre.
(I will live in this city forever.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I will live in this city forever.
facts.explanation: viviré — future simple of vivir (yo). vivir + é = viviré. -ir verbs: same pattern, attach to full infinitive. viviré, vivirás, vivirá, viviremos, viviréis, vivirán.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} los deberes esta tarde.
(I will do my homework this afternoon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I will do my homework this afternoon.
facts.explanation: haré — future of hacer (yo). Irregular stem har- + é = haré. Same stem as conditional: haría. Key memory link: if the conditional is haría, the future is haré (same stem, different endings).

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Qué {___} este fin de semana?
(What will you do this weekend?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: What will you do this weekend?
facts.explanation: harás — future of hacer (tú). har- + ás = harás. Full paradigm: haré, harás, hará, haremos, haréis, harán. The classic question ¿Qué harás? tests both the irregular stem and future tense usage.

facts.quizQuestion:
Le {___} la verdad mañana.
(I will tell him the truth tomorrow.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I will tell him the truth tomorrow.
facts.explanation: diré — future of decir (yo). dir- + é = diré. decir and hacer both drop their infinitive ending and contract: dir-, har-. diré, dirás, dirá, diremos, diréis, dirán.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} que trabajar el sábado.
(I will have to work on Saturday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I will have to work on Saturday.
facts.explanation: tendré — future of tener (yo). tener inserts -d-: tendr- + é = tendré. Same stem group: tener → tendr-, venir → vendr-, salir → saldr-, poner → pondr-. tendré que + infinitive = will have to...

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} venir mañana?
(Will you be able to come tomorrow?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Will you be able to come tomorrow?
facts.explanation: podrás — future of poder (tú). poder drops -e-: podr- + ás = podrás. Common question structure: ¿Podrás...? (future ability). Contrast with ¿Podrías...? (conditional, polite request).

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} a la reunión?
(Will you come to the meeting?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Will you come to the meeting?
facts.explanation: vendrás — future of venir (tú). vendr- + ás = vendrás. vendr- stem: vendré, vendrás, vendrá, vendremos, vendréis, vendrán. Don't confuse with conditional: vendría (same stem, -ía endings).

facts.quizQuestion:
Si estudias, {___} el examen.
(If you study, you will pass the exam.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you study, you will pass the exam.
facts.explanation: aprobarás — future of aprobar in a type 1 si-clause. Pattern: si + present indicative + future simple. The si clause uses present indicative (not subjunctive). Result clause uses future. Si estudias → you study (condition), aprobarás → you will pass (result).

facts.quizQuestion:
Si no llueve, {___} al parque.
(If it doesn't rain, we'll go to the park.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If it doesn't rain, we'll go to the park.
facts.explanation: iremos — future of ir (nosotros). Si clause: si no llueve (present indicative). Result: iremos (future). Si-clauses type 1 express real/likely conditions. Contrast type 2 (B2+): si no lloviera, iríamos (imperfect subj + conditional).

facts.quizQuestion:
Cuando llegué, ya {___} comido.
(When I arrived, they had already eaten.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: When I arrived, they had already eaten.
facts.explanation: habían — imperfect of haber (ellos), used to form pluperfect (had done). Pluperfect = había/habías/había/habíamos/habíais/habían + past participle. Expresses an action that happened before another past action. Ya habían comido = they had already eaten.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ya {___} llegado cuando llamaste.
(I had already arrived when you called.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I had already arrived when you called.
facts.explanation: había — imperfect of haber (yo/él/ella). Ya había llegado = I had already arrived. The pluperfect is used for the earlier of two past actions. Ya (already) + pluperfect is a very common B1 pattern.

facts.quizQuestion:
No {___} dormido bien.
(You hadn't slept well.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: You hadn't slept well.
facts.explanation: habías — imperfect of haber (tú). No habías dormido bien = you hadn't slept well (past state before another point in time). habías is used only for tú in the pluperfect.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} terminado antes de las ocho.
(We had finished before eight.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We had finished before eight.
facts.explanation: habíamos — imperfect of haber (nosotros). Habíamos terminado = we had finished. The pluperfect is used to show one past action happened before another. Habíamos terminado → antes de las ocho (time reference).

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} visto esa película antes?
(Had you seen that film before?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Had you seen that film before?
facts.explanation: habías — imperfect of haber (tú) for pluperfect. ¿Habías visto esa película? = Had you seen that film? A classic B1 pluperfect question using ver (irregular PP: visto). Note: ¿Has visto? (present perfect) vs. ¿Habías visto? (pluperfect).

facts.quizQuestion:
He {___} la ventana.
(I have opened the window.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I have opened the window.
facts.explanation: abierto — irregular past participle of abrir. abrir → abierto (not *abrido). Common usage: He abierto (present perfect), La puerta estaba abierta (adjective), Tengo la ventana abierta (result state).

facts.quizQuestion:
La puerta estaba {___}.
(The door was open.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The door was open.
facts.grammarPointLabel: irregular PP as adjective: abrir → abierto/a
facts.explanation: abierta — past participle of abrir used as adjective. As an adjective, it agrees in gender/number: abierto (masc), abierta (fem), abiertos, abiertas. La puerta (feminine) → abierta.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Has {___} la carta?
(Have you written the letter?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Have you written the letter?
facts.explanation: escrito — irregular past participle of escribir. escribir → escrito (not *escribido). Also: describir → descrito (same pattern). Useful sentence: El libro está escrito en español.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Has {___} los deberes?
(Have you done your homework?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Have you done your homework?
facts.explanation: hecho — irregular past participle of hacer. hacer → hecho (not *hacido). One of the most common irregular participles: ¿Qué has hecho? (What have you done?), está hecho (it's done), de hecho (in fact).

facts.quizQuestion:
El pájaro había {___}.
(The bird had died.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The bird had died.
facts.explanation: muerto — irregular past participle of morir. morir → muerto (not *morido). Used in present perfect and pluperfect: Ha muerto (has died), había muerto (had died). As adjective: estar muerto/a (to be dead).

facts.quizQuestion:
He {___} el vaso sin querer.
(I have broken the glass by accident.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I have broken the glass by accident.
facts.explanation: roto — irregular past participle of romper. romper → roto (not *rompido). Adjective usage: La ventana está rota. Present perfect: He roto el plato. Common B1 vocab item paired with accidental actions.

facts.quizQuestion:
La ventana estaba {___}.
(The window was broken.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The window was broken.
facts.grammarPointLabel: irregular PP as adjective: romper → roto/a
facts.explanation: rota — past participle of romper used as feminine adjective. La ventana (fem) → rota. Note agreement: el cristal roto (masc), la ventana rota (fem).

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Has {___} el problema?
(Have you solved the problem?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Have you solved the problem?
facts.explanation: resuelto — irregular past participle of resolver. resolver → resuelto (not *resolvido). Same pattern: volver → vuelto, absolver → absuelto. Common in academic/professional contexts.

facts.quizQuestion:
La mesa está {___} de polvo.
(The table is covered in dust.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The table is covered in dust.
facts.explanation: cubierto — irregular past participle of cubrir. cubrir → cubierto (not *cubrido). Same pattern: descubrir → descubierto, encubrir → encubierto. La mesa está cubierta (adjective, agrees with fem noun).

facts.quizQuestion:
He {___} las llaves en la mesa.
(I have put the keys on the table.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I have put the keys on the table.
facts.explanation: puesto — irregular past participle of poner. poner → puesto (not *ponido). Also: componer → compuesto, imponer → impuesto, proponer → propuesto. He puesto = I have put/placed.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Has {___} del trabajo?
(Have you returned from work?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Have you returned from work?
facts.explanation: vuelto — irregular past participle of volver. volver → vuelto (not *volvido). Same pattern: resolver → resuelto, devolver → devuelto. Ha vuelto = he/she has returned.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} dije ayer.
(I told him/her it yesterday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I told him/her it yesterday.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: le lo → se lo
facts.explanation: se lo — when indirect object le or les precedes direct object lo/la/los/las, le/les changes to se. Rule: le/les + lo/la/los/las → se + lo/la/los/las. se lo dije = I told it to him/her. Never: *le lo dije.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} has explicado?
(Have you explained it to him/her?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Have you explained it to him/her?
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: se lo (3rd person IO)
facts.explanation: se lo — indirect object le (to him/her) + direct object lo (it) → se lo. ¿Se lo has explicado? = Have you explained it to him/her? Both pronouns come before the conjugated verb haber.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} mandé por correo.
(I sent it to him/her by mail.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I sent it to him/her by mail.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: se la (feminine DO)
facts.explanation: se la — le (to him/her) + la (it, feminine) → se la. The direct object pronoun (lo/la/los/las) agrees in gender with the noun it replaces. La carta (f) → la. Se la mandé = I sent it (the letter, f) to him/her.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} presté ayer.
(I lent them to him/her yesterday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I lent them to him/her yesterday.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: se los (plural masculine DO)
facts.explanation: se los — le (to him/her) + los (them, masc. plural) → se los. Los libros (m pl) → los. Se los presté = I lent them (the books) to him/her. Plural direct objects: se los (masc), se las (fem).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} traje de vacaciones.
(I brought them to him/her from vacation.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I brought them to him/her from vacation.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: se las (plural feminine DO)
facts.explanation: se las — le (to him/her) + las (them, fem. plural) → se las. Las fotos (f pl) → las. Se las traje = I brought them (the photos, f pl) to him/her.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} dijo mi madre.
(My mother told me it.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: My mother told me it.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: me lo (1st person IO)
facts.explanation: me lo — indirect object me (to me) + direct object lo (it) → me lo. When 1st/2nd person is the indirect object, no change: me lo, te lo, nos lo (unlike 3rd person where le/les → se). Me lo dijo = she told it to me.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿{___} puedes explicar?
(Can you explain it to me?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Can you explain it to me?
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: me lo before conjugated verb
facts.explanation: me lo — both pronouns come before the conjugated verb puedes. Order: IO (me/te/se/nos) + DO (lo/la/los/las). ¿Me lo puedes explicar? is equivalent to ¿Puedes explicármelo? (attached to infinitive).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} mandó ayer.
(He/she sent it to me yesterday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He/she sent it to me yesterday.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: me la (feminine DO)
facts.explanation: me la — me (to me) + la (it, feminine). La invitación (f) → la. Me la mandó = he/she sent it (the invitation, f) to me.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} juro.
(I swear it to you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I swear it to you.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: te lo (2nd person IO)
facts.explanation: te lo — te (to you) + lo (it) → te lo. No change needed for 2nd person: te + lo = te lo (unlike 3rd person le lo → se lo). Te lo juro is an emotional fixed expression.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ya {___} dije.
(I already told you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I already told you.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: te lo (before conjugated verb)
facts.explanation: te lo — te (to you) + lo (it). Ya te lo dije = I already told you (it). This is an extremely common spoken Spanish expression. Pronouns come before the conjugated verb dije.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} enviaré mañana.
(I'll send it to you tomorrow.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'll send it to you tomorrow.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: te la (feminine DO + future)
facts.explanation: te la — te (to you) + la (it, feminine). With future tense: Te la enviaré = I'll send it (e.g., la información, f) to you tomorrow. Pronouns always stay before conjugated verb, even in future.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} explicó el profesor.
(The teacher explained it to us.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The teacher explained it to us.
facts.grammarPointLabel: double object pronoun: nos lo (1st person plural IO)
facts.explanation: nos lo — nos (to us) + lo (it). Nos lo explicó = he/she explained it to us. 1st person plural: nos lo, nos la, nos los, nos las (no change, unlike 3rd person se).

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero {___} ahora.
(I want to give it to you now.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want to give it to you now.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to infinitive → dártelo
facts.explanation: dártelo — pronouns can attach to infinitives: dar + te + lo = dártelo. Or they can go before quiero: Te lo quiero dar. Both are correct. With attachment, accent on dar: dár-te-lo. IO before DO: te (IO) + lo (DO).

facts.quizQuestion:
Necesito {___} hoy.
(I need to send it to him/her today.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I need to send it to him/her today.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to infinitive → mandárselo
facts.explanation: mandárselo — mandar + se + lo = mandárselo. When pronouns attach to infinitive: accent mark added on the stressed syllable of the verb. le + lo → se before attaching. Alternative: necesito sElo mandar.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}, por favor.
(Give it to me, please.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Give it to me, please.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to affirmative imperative → dámelo
facts.explanation: dámelo — da (tú imperative of dar) + me + lo = dámelo. Pronouns must attach to affirmative imperatives. Accent on the imperative stem: dÁme-lo. Never *me lo da (imperative — would be present indicative). Negative: no me lo des.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} al profesor.
(Give it to the teacher.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Give it to the teacher.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to usted affirmative imperative → déselo
facts.explanation: déselo — dé (usted imperative of dar) + se + lo = déselo. Usted imperatives also attach pronouns: dÉse-lo. le al profesor → se (because before lo). So: dé + se + lo = déselo. Formal instruction.

facts.quizQuestion:
El libro {___} leí es muy bueno.
(The book that I read is very good.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The book that I read is very good.
facts.explanation: que — most common relative pronoun in Spanish, used for both people and things. El libro que leí = the book that I read. que never changes form (unlike English who/which/that). Used after both animate and inanimate antecedents.

facts.quizQuestion:
La persona {___} llamó es mi amigo.
(The person who called is my friend.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The person who called is my friend.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: que (for people, subject position)
facts.explanation: que — used for people when they are the subject of the relative clause. La persona que llamó = the person who called. Note: quien can also be used for people, but que is more common in spoken Spanish.

facts.quizQuestion:
La casa {___} compramos es grande.
(The house that we bought is big.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The house that we bought is big.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: que (object of relative clause)
facts.explanation: que — relative pronoun used when the antecedent (la casa) is the object of the relative verb. La casa que compramos = the house that we bought. Unlike English, Spanish never omits que: *la casa compramos is wrong.

facts.quizQuestion:
La persona con {___} trabajo es simpática.
(The person with whom I work is nice.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The person with whom I work is nice.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: quien (after preposition, for people)
facts.explanation: quien — used for people, especially after prepositions: con quien, a quien, de quien. La persona con quien trabajo = the person with whom I work. que cannot follow a preposition directly (in standard Spanish).

facts.quizQuestion:
El chico a {___} vi es tu hermano.
(The boy whom I saw is your brother.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The boy whom I saw is your brother.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: quien (object of verb with personal a)
facts.explanation: quien — after personal a when referring to people: a quien vi = whom I saw. The personal a before people in object position persists before quien. Contrast: El chico que vi (also correct, but more informal).

facts.quizQuestion:
La ciudad {___} vivo es Madrid.
(The city where I live is Madrid.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The city where I live is Madrid.
facts.explanation: donde — relative pronoun used for places. La ciudad donde vivo = the city where I live. donde is used when the antecedent is a place. Contrast: en que (more formal, same meaning): la ciudad en que vivo.

facts.quizQuestion:
El restaurante {___} comemos siempre está lleno.
(The restaurant where we always eat is full.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The restaurant where we always eat is full.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: donde (for places, non-proper nouns)
facts.explanation: donde — used for any place antecedent, not just cities. El restaurante donde comemos = the restaurant where we eat. donde here = in which. Adverb donde becomes a relative pronoun when it follows a noun antecedent.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} dices es verdad.
(What you say is true.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: What you say is true.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: lo que (abstract antecedent — what)
facts.explanation: lo que — used when there is no specific antecedent (equivalent to 'what' = 'that which'). Lo que dices = what you say / that which you say. lo que is the subject or object with no preceding noun. Very common at B1+.

facts.quizQuestion:
No entiendo {___} quieres decir.
(I don't understand what you mean.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't understand what you mean.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: lo que (object position, embedded clause)
facts.explanation: lo que — lo que quieres decir = what you want to say / what you mean. lo que here is the object of entiendo. In English this is 'what', in Spanish always lo que (not *qué in indirect statements).

facts.quizQuestion:
Haz {___} quieras.
(Do whatever you want.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Do whatever you want.
facts.grammarPointLabel: relative clause: lo que (whatever = lo que + subjunctive)
facts.explanation: lo que — Haz lo que quieras = Do whatever you want. lo que + subjunctive = whatever. This combines relative pronoun lo que with the present subjunctive for open/indefinite reference. Common in everyday speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llegar a casa.
(I've just arrived home.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I've just arrived home.
facts.explanation: acabo de — acabar de + infinitive = to have just done. Acabo de llegar = I've just arrived (moments ago). Formation: conjugated acabar + de + infinitive. This B1 periphrasis precisely expresses recent completion.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} salir.
(He/she has just left.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He/she has just left.
facts.explanation: acaba de — él/ella form of acabar + de + infinitive. Acaba de salir = he/she has just left. Present tense: acabo, acabas, acaba, acabamos, acabáis, acaban + de + infinitive. Very natural Spanish for recent events.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} terminar el proyecto.
(We've just finished the project.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We've just finished the project.
facts.explanation: acabamos de — nosotros form. Acabamos de terminar = we've just finished. Note: acabamos de (nosotros) vs. acababan de (ellos imperfect) — both frequently used. Imperfect: Acababan de llegar cuando llamé.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llamar esta mañana.
(He called again this morning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He called again this morning.
facts.explanation: volvió a — volver a + infinitive = to do something again. Volvió a llamar = he called again. Any conjugated form of volver + a + infinitive expresses repetition. Present: vuelvo a, vuelves a... Preterite: volví a, volviste a...

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} intentarlo.
(I'll try again.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'll try again.
facts.grammarPointLabel: volver a + infinitive (do again): yo present
facts.explanation: vuelvo a — present tense yo form of volver + a + infinitive. Vuelvo a intentarlo = I'm trying again / I'll try again. Often expresses determination or frustration. Note stem change: volver → vuelvo (o→ue).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estudiando español.
(I'm still studying Spanish.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm still studying Spanish.
facts.explanation: sigo — seguir + gerundio = to keep doing / to still be doing. Sigo estudiando = I'm still studying. seguir has a stem change (e→i): sigo, sigues, sigue, seguimos, seguís, siguen. gerundio: estudiando (regular -ar).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} lloviendo.
(It's still raining.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's still raining.
facts.explanation: sigue — él/ella form of seguir + gerundio. Sigue lloviendo = it's still raining. Common in weather expressions. Note: llover → lloviendo (regular -er gerund). Seguir imperfect: seguía lloviendo (it kept raining).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} trabajando cuando llegué.
(They were still working when I arrived.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: They were still working when I arrived.
facts.explanation: seguían — imperfect of seguir (ellos) + gerundio. Seguían trabajando = they kept working / were still working (ongoing state in past). Imperfect of seguir: seguía, seguías, seguía, seguíamos, seguíais, seguían.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tres años estudiando español.
(I've been studying Spanish for three years.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I've been studying Spanish for three years.
facts.grammarPointLabel: llevar + time + gerundio (have been doing for): yo
facts.explanation: llevo — llevar + time expression + gerundio = to have been doing for [time]. Llevo tres años estudiando = I've been studying for three years. This replaces the English 'have been doing' construction. Amount of time + gerundio.

facts.quizQuestion:
¿Cuánto tiempo {___} esperando?
(How long have you been waiting?)
facts.sentenceTranslation: How long have you been waiting?
facts.explanation: llevas — tú form of llevar. ¿Cuánto tiempo llevas esperando? = How long have you been waiting? The question asks about duration. Answer: Llevo dos horas esperando. llevar (yo→llevo, tú→llevas, él→lleva).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} dos horas trabajando.
(He's been working for two hours.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He's been working for two hours.
facts.explanation: lleva — él/ella form. Lleva dos horas trabajando = he's been working for two hours. Pattern: Subject + lleva/llevas/llevo... + time expression + gerundio. The time expression comes between llevar and gerundio.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} una semana aquí.
(We've been here for a week.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We've been here for a week.
facts.explanation: llevamos — nosotros form. Llevamos una semana aquí = we've been here for a week. Note: in this case no gerundio follows because 'estar aquí' is implied, not an active action. Llevar can express duration without a gerundio in some contexts.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es necesario que {___} todos presentes.
(It's necessary that we all be present.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's necessary that we all be present.
facts.explanation: estemos — present subjunctive of estar (nosotros). Es necesario que triggers subjunctive. Paradigm: esté, estés, esté, estemos, estéis, estén.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quiero que {___} contentos.
(I want them to be happy.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I want them to be happy.
facts.explanation: estén — present subjunctive of estar (ellos/ustedes). Quiero que requires subjunctive. estar subjunctive: esté, estés, esté, estemos, estéis, estén.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} solo, señor.
(Don't go alone, sir.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't go alone, sir.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative usted imperative: ir → no vaya
facts.explanation: no vaya — negative usted imperative of ir. Uses subjunctive: vaya (usted subjunctive of ir). Affirmative: vaya usted. Negative: no vaya usted. The vaya form serves both purposes.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} ruido, por favor.
(Please don't make noise.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Please don't make noise.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative ustedes imperative: hacer → no hagan
facts.explanation: no hagan — negative ustedes imperative of hacer. Uses subjunctive: hagan (ustedes). No hagan ruido = please don't make noise. Common in public spaces (libraries, cinemas).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} sin paraguas — llueve mucho.
(Don't go out without an umbrella — it's raining a lot.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't go out without an umbrella — it's raining a lot.
facts.grammarPointLabel: negative usted imperative: salir → no salga
facts.explanation: no salga — negative usted imperative of salir. salir subjunctive: salga. Usted: no salga. Contrast with tú: no salgas. The formal register uses -a ending for -ir verbs (salga, no salga).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} su nombre aquí, por favor.
(Put your name here, please.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Put your name here, please.
facts.explanation: ponga — affirmative usted imperative of poner. Uses subjunctive: ponga. Tú affirmative: pon vs. usted: ponga. Common in formal instructions and forms.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} por la puerta de la izquierda.
(Exit through the left door.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Exit through the left door.
facts.explanation: salga — affirmative usted imperative of salir. Uses subjunctive: salga. Tú affirmative: sal vs. usted: salga. Common in formal directions and signage.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} al médico mañana.
(Go to the doctor tomorrow.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Go to the doctor tomorrow.
facts.explanation: vaya — affirmative usted imperative of ir (irregular). ir subjunctive: vaya. Tú affirmative: ve vs. usted: vaya. Vaya is also used as an exclamation (Vaya! = Well!/What a...!).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} el ejercicio número tres.
(Do exercise number three.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Do exercise number three.
facts.explanation: hagan — affirmative ustedes imperative of hacer. Uses subjunctive (hagan). Common in classroom instructions. Contrast: usted → haga / ustedes → hagan.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} cuidado con el tráfico.
(Be careful with traffic.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Be careful with traffic.
facts.explanation: tengan — affirmative ustedes imperative of tener. Uses subjunctive (tengan). Tengan cuidado is the formal/plural equivalent of Ten cuidado (tú) / Tenga cuidado (usted).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} directamente al aeropuerto.
(Go directly to the airport.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Go directly to the airport.
facts.explanation: vayan — affirmative ustedes imperative of ir. Uses subjunctive (vayan). ir imperative across persons: ve (tú), vaya (usted), vayamos (nosotros), vayan (ustedes).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} optimistas.
(Let's be optimistic.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's be optimistic.
facts.explanation: seamos — nosotros imperative of ser. Uses subjunctive (seamos). Let's be = seamos. The irregular subjunctive form of ser also serves as the nosotros imperative.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} paciencia.
(Let's be patient.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's be patient.
facts.explanation: tengamos — nosotros imperative of tener. Uses subjunctive (tengamos). tener subjunctive nosotros: tengamos. Tengamos paciencia = let's be patient / let's have patience.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} un descanso.
(Let's take a break.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Let's take a break.
facts.explanation: hagamos — nosotros imperative of hacer. Uses subjunctive (hagamos). Hagamos un descanso = let's take a break. hacer nosotros subjunctive: hagamos.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} más si tuviera tiempo.
(I would study more if I had time.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I would study more if I had time.
facts.explanation: estudiaría — conditional of estudiar (yo). estudiar + ía = estudiaría. Regular: any -ar/-er/-ir verb: add -ía to full infinitive. This constructs a hypothetical situation.

facts.quizQuestion:
Con más dinero, {___} menos horas.
(With more money, they would work fewer hours.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: With more money, they would work fewer hours.
facts.explanation: trabajarían — conditional of trabajar (ellos). trabajar + ían = trabajarían. Ellos conditional ends in -ían (with accent). Expresses a hypothetical result.

facts.quizQuestion:
Mañana {___} con el director.
(Tomorrow they will speak with the director.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Tomorrow they will speak with the director.
facts.explanation: hablarán — future of hablar (ellos/ellas). hablar + án = hablarán. ellos future ends in -án (with accent). Full paradigm: hablaré, hablarás, hablará, hablaremos, hablaréis, hablarán.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si consigues el trabajo, {___} mucho.
(If you get the job, you'll work a lot.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you get the job, you'll work a lot.
facts.explanation: trabajarás — future of trabajar (tú). trabajar + ás = trabajarás. This is also a si-clause type 1: si consigues (present) + trabajarás (future).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tienes tiempo, llámame.
(If you have time, call me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you have time, call me.
facts.explanation: si — the conjunction si (if) introduces type 1 conditional clauses. Structure: si + present indicative + present/future/imperative. Si tienes tiempo (present indicative in condition), llámame (imperative in result). Si NEVER takes subjunctive in type 1.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} comes bien, te sentirás mejor.
(If you eat well, you'll feel better.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you eat well, you'll feel better.
facts.explanation: si — Si comes bien (condition: present indicative) → te sentirás mejor (result: future). Type 1 si-clauses express real, likely conditions. Compare: cuando comes bien, te sientes mejor (habitual).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} no estudias, suspenderás.
(If you don't study, you'll fail.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you don't study, you'll fail.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 1: negative condition → negative result
facts.explanation: si — negative type 1 si-clause. Si no estudias (negative present) → suspenderás (future). The si-clause can come first or second: Suspenderás si no estudias. Contrast: si no estudiases, suspenderías (type 2, B2+).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} me llamas, te ayudaré.
(If you call me, I'll help you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you call me, I'll help you.
facts.explanation: si — Si me llamas (condition: present indicative) → te ayudaré (result: future). The future tense in the result clause is characteristic of type 1 si-clauses. Key contrast: si + present (type 1) vs. si + imperfect subjunctive (type 2, B2).

facts.quizQuestion:
Está {___} ahora.
(He's explaining it to me now.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He's explaining it to me now.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to gerund → explicándomelo
facts.explanation: explicándomelo — explicando + me + lo = explicándomelo. When pronouns attach to gerunds, add an accent on the stressed syllable: explicÁNdomelo. IO (me) before DO (lo). Alternative: me lo está explicando.

facts.quizQuestion:
Siguió {___}.
(He kept telling it to me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He kept telling it to me.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to gerund → diciéndomelo
facts.explanation: diciéndomelo — diciendo + me + lo = diciéndomelo. decir gerund: diciendo (stem change e→i). With attached pronouns: diciÉNdomelo. Used with seguir + gerundio periphrasis.

facts.quizQuestion:
Voy a {___}.
(I'm going to explain it to you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm going to explain it to you.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to infinitive → explicártelo
facts.explanation: explicártelo — explicar + te + lo = explicártelo. With ir a + infinitive, pronouns can attach to the infinitive or go before ir: Voy a explicártelo = Te lo voy a explicar. Accent on verb: explicÁRtelo.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}.
(Tell it to me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Tell it to me.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pronoun placement: attached to usted affirmative imperative → dígamelo
facts.explanation: dígamelo — diga (usted imperative of decir) + me + lo = dígamelo. Accent on diga → dÍgamelo. Usted imperatives attach pronouns: dígame (tell me), déselo (give it to him/her), dígamelo (tell it to me).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llueve, nos quedamos en casa.
(If it rains, we'll stay at home.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If it rains, we'll stay at home.
facts.explanation: si — Si llueve (condition: present indicative) → nos quedamos en casa (result: present or future). Type 1 si-clauses can also have the present indicative in the result (habit/general truth). Si + present → never subjunctive in type 1.

chainThemes.description: Affirmative imperative (usted/ustedes/nosotros) + negative imperative all persons (uses subjunctive)

chainThemes.name: Simple Conditional
chainThemes.description: Condicional simple — hablaría, comería + irregular stems (haría, diría, tendría, podría)

chainThemes.name: Future Simple
chainThemes.description: Futuro simple — hablaré, comeré + irregular stems (haré, diré, tendré) + si-clauses type 1

chainThemes.name: Pluperfect & Past Participles
chainThemes.description: Pluscuamperfecto (había + PP) + irregular past participles B1 (abierto, escrito, muerto, roto)

chainThemes.name: Double Object Pronouns
chainThemes.description: se lo/la/los/las + me/te/nos lo/la + pronoun placement with infinitives, gerunds, imperatives

chainThemes.name: Relative Clauses
chainThemes.description: que / quien / donde / lo que — basic relative clause structures

chainThemes.name: Verbal Periphrases
chainThemes.description: acabar de + inf (just did) / volver a + inf (do again) / seguir + gerund / llevar + time + gerund
